summary:
The War of the Spanish Succession (1701–1714 AD) was a major European conflict triggered by the question of who would inherit the vast Spanish Empire after the childless King Charles II of Spain died in 1700, naming Louis XIV's grandson Philip of Anjou as his heir — a succession that, if accepted, would have united France and Spain under Bourbon rule and given France overwhelming dominance over European and colonial affairs. Britain, the Dutch Republic, Austria, and most German states formed a Grand Alliance to prevent this outcome, while France and Spain resisted; the resulting war was fought across Europe (Germany, Italy, the Spanish Netherlands, the Iberian Peninsula) and in the Americas and Asia.

The war produced some of the 18th century's most celebrated military engagements: the [[event:2392|Battle of Blenheim]] (1704), where Marlborough and Eugene of Savoy shattered a Franco-Bavarian army in the greatest Allied victory of the war; Ramillies (1706); Oudenarde (1708). Spain itself became a theater of war between Habsburg and Bourbon claimants. The war ended with the [[event:2383|Treaty of Utrecht]] (1713–1714), which produced a pragmatic settlement: Philip V kept Spain and its colonies (creating the Bourbon monarchy that persists today) but renounced the French succession, preventing the feared Franco-Spanish union. Britain gained Gibraltar, Minorca, Newfoundland, Acadia, and the lucrative asiento (the right to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish colonies). Austria received the Spanish Netherlands and Italian territories. Utrecht is often seen as the foundation of 18th-century European balance-of-power diplomacy — an effort to prevent any single power from achieving continental hegemony.
